title: Equality, truthiness and coercion

publication.changeSummary: Technology-specific JavaScript: Language and Browser Applications draft for Equality, truthiness and coercion; independent standards, browser, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: ECMAScript Language Specification; living specification accessed 2026-07; provisions applicable to Equality, truthiness and coercion
sources.claim: JavaScript operators can perform explicit or implicit conversion according to operator-specific abstract operations.

canonicalExplanation.summary: JavaScript operators can perform explicit or implicit conversion according to operator-specific abstract operations.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: Number('12') explicitly produces 12 while '12' + 3 concatenates to '123'. Your goal is to predict and repair equality, concatenation and truthiness defects. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Scripts and console; pf state expressions; Primitive values and typeof. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Technology rule
sections.body: JavaScript operators can perform explicit or implicit conversion according to operator-specific abstract operations. Apply this rule to the actual markup, stylesheet, JavaScript, or component state rather than memorizing its name.

sections.heading: Boundary and browser reality
sections.body: Equality, addition, truthiness, property keys, and relational comparison use different coercion paths. This draft therefore rejects: The plus operator always performs numeric addition when one operand is a number.

includes: Prefer explicit conversion and strict equality while understanding common coercion rules.
Predict and repair equality, concatenation and truthiness defects.
excludes: Equality, addition, truthiness, property keys, and relational comparison use different coercion paths.

outcomes: Predict and repair equality, concatenation and truthiness defects.

examples.title: Equality, truthiness and coercion: concrete case
examples.situation: Number('12') explicitly produces 12 while '12' + 3 concatenates to '123'.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the exact artifact and starting state: Number('12') explicitly produces 12 while '12' + 3 concatenates to '123'.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: String participation can make + concatenate; convert inputs explicitly before arithmetic.
examples.result: The result follows because String participation can make + concatenate; convert inputs explicitly before arithmetic.

examples.title: Equality, truthiness and coercion: boundary case
examples.situation: Equality, addition, truthiness, property keys, and relational comparison use different coercion paths.

Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Reject the misconception: The plus operator always performs numeric addition when one operand is a number.
examples.result: The boundary requires this correction: String participation can make + concatenate; convert inputs explicitly before arithmetic.

limitations: Human coordination does not reproduce coercion, lexical scope, microtasks, prototypes, or browser security boundaries.
